name: Pozas Pígalo by the Castillo de Sibirana from Luesia
name_original: Pozas Pígalo por el Castillo de Sibirana desde Luesia
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Luesia traverse the Pre-Pyrenees mountains and the Sierra Santo Domingo, offering varied terrain for cyclists. The landscape features a mix of challenging ascents and descents, dense forests, and open areas with yellow grasses. Geological formations like the Gypsum Ravine and natural river pools such as the Pígalo Pools are notable features along many routes. Best touring cycling routes around Luesia The most popular touring cycling route is Pozas Pígalo by the Castillo de Sibirana from Luesia, a 16.9 miles (27.2 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 7 minutes to complete. This route passes by the Pígalo Pools, offering a natural stop. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Asín and Luesia Castles Route, a difficult 32.6 miles (52.5 km) path. This route incorporates historical landmarks, including the 10th-century Romanesque Castle of Luesia. Local touring cyclists also love the Biel Castle – Refugio de la Artica loop from Biel, a 16.4 miles (26.4 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 1 minute. Touring cycling around Luesia is defined by Pre-Pyrenean mountain ranges, historical castles, and natural river pools. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging routes. The routes in Luesia are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 40 reviews. More than 50 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Luesia's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Luesia?
A: There are over 15 touring cycling routes around Luesia, offering a variety of experiences for cyclists. These routes traverse the Pre-Pyrenees mountains and the Sierra Santo Domingo, providing diverse terrain.

Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty can I expect on touring cycling routes in Luesia?
A: The routes around Luesia offer a mix of challenging ascents and descents, characteristic of the Pre-Pyrenean mountains. While there are no easy routes listed, you'll find 4 moderate and 13 difficult routes. The terrain includes dense forests, open areas with yellow grasses, and unique geological formations like the Gypsum Ravine.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Luesia?
A: While the region is known for its more challenging Pre-Pyrenean terrain, there are moderate routes available. For a moderate option that includes a refreshing stop, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994085469/pozas-pigalo-by-the-castillo-de-sibirana-from-luesia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozas Pígalo by the Castillo de Sibirana from Luesia&lt;/a&gt; route, which passes by the Pígalo Pools, a natural river pool area equipped with picnic spots.

Q: What natural features can I explore along the touring cycling routes?
A: Luesia's routes are rich in natural beauty. You can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2716013" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pígalo Pools&lt;/a&gt;, natural river pools perfect for a refreshing break, or witness the unique geological formations of the Gypsum Ravine. The routes also offer stunning panoramic views from elevated points like the Puy Fonguera Summit, known for its rich flora and fauna.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or castles to visit while cycling?
A: Absolutely! The region is dotted with historical sites. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994056204/asin-and-luesia-castles-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Asín and Luesia Castles Route&lt;/a&gt; specifically incorporates historical landmarks, including the impressive 10th-century Romanesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5271588" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Luesia Castle and Church of El Salvador&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable castles you might encounter include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5271556" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Biel Castle&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5332085" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Uncastillo Historic Center&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Luesia?
A: The diverse vegetation and varied terrain of Luesia mean that different seasons offer unique experiences. While specific recommendations depend on personal preference, the warmer months are ideal for enjoying natural stops like the Pígalo Pools. The region's landscapes, with yellow grass, blue skies, and dense forests, are beautiful throughout the year, but spring and autumn generally offer more comfortable temperatures for cycling.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31502342" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Biel Castle – Refugio de la Artica loop from Biel&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 16.4-mile (26.4 km) route through mountainous terrain. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24550598" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sibirana Castle – Pígalo Pools loop from Uncastillo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Luesia?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 40 reviews. Cyclists often praise the stunning landscapes, the rich flora and fauna, and the combination of challenging mountain routes with serene natural stops and rich historical sites. The varied trails cater to different preferences, ensuring both physical activity and cultural immersion.

Q: Are there any places to stop for refreshments or accommodation along the routes?
A: While specific cafes or pubs directly on every route are not detailed, the town of Luesia itself, with its narrow, cobbled streets and traditional squares, offers opportunities for stops. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5284691" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refugio de la Artica&lt;/a&gt; is a hut that can serve as a resting point on some routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31502342" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Biel Castle – Refugio de la Artica loop from Biel&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Luesia?
A: The natural landscapes and varied terrain of Luesia are generally welcoming for outdoor activities. While specific dog-friendly regulations for each route are not provided, many natural areas in the Pre-Pyrenees allow dogs. It's always advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near livestock, and to ensure they are well-behaved and don't disturb other users or the environment.

Q: Are there any routes that offer particularly scenic viewpoints?
A: Many routes in Luesia are known for their high scenic level. Routes leading to the Puy Fonguera Summit are particularly noted for stunning landscapes and panoramic views. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5315914" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Biel Castle and Church&lt;/a&gt; highlight offers a picturesque vista that can be incorporated into your cycling tour.

Q: What is the longest touring cycling route in the area?
A: One of the longer and more challenging routes is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994056204/asin-and-luesia-castles-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Asín and Luesia Castles Route&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 32.6 miles (52.5 km). This difficult path offers a substantial ride while incorporating historical landmarks.
tips: We are in the Aragonese town of Luesia, a picturesque town where one of the oldest fortresses in Aragon is located or the Church of El Salvador, located at the top of the municipality.

On our way along the track from Luesia to the Pígalo pools, we come across the castle of Sibirana built to control the passage from Uncastillo and from Luesia through the Onsella river valley. In addition to the castle, there are remains of the hermitage of Santa Quiteria, both in the Aragonese Romanesque style and of a dozen houses at the foot of the castle on its south face.

We are in the Pígalo pools, ideal for warm summer days where we can take a dip. If we go upstream, we will find more less crowded pools, since this one is quite popular and soon fills up with people.
If we want to leave the car in the car park, we must book on the Luesia town hall website as they control access. There are picnic areas to eat and a bar at the campsite.

waypoints.name: Luesia Castle and Church of El Salvador
